column1: What The Rep Number Actually Means
Reference material for the app, and for anything user facing. Reps are a proxy for load. That is the whole idea.

column3: Maximal strength. Almost entirely neural. The muscle barely changes, the nervous system learns to recruit it
column4: Heavy, slow, total concentration. The bar moves whether you like it or not
column5: Testing a max, or peaking a strength block. Rare for a student population
column6: Highest technical and injury risk. Needs a spotter and a lift the user has done for months. Not for a remote app to prescribe casually

column3: Strength with a little size. The best all round strength range
column4: Hard from rep one. Speed drops noticeably on the last rep
column5: The default strength range. Where a compound lift should live in a strength block
column6: Fatiguing on the joints and the nervous system. Needs long rest, so sessions run long

column3: The classic hypertrophy range. Enough load for tension, enough reps for volume
column4: Burning by rep eight, genuinely hard by rep ten
column5: The default for most users on most exercises. If in doubt, prescribe here
column6: It is not magic. It is popular because it balances load and volume efficiently, not because the muscle counts reps

column3: Hypertrophy plus muscular endurance. Equal growth to the mid range IF taken close to failure
column4: A deep burn. The last few reps are grim and slow
column5: Isolation work, bodyweight exercises, anything where load cannot be added easily
column6: Most people stop well short of failure here because it is uncomfortable. Under effort, this range does very little

column3: Muscular endurance, work capacity, local conditioning
column4: Cardiovascular before it is muscular. Lungs give up before the muscle
column6: Poor return on time for both strength and size. The set ends because you are out of breath, not because the muscle failed

column1: Rules That Override The Table Above

column2: For muscle growth, a set of 8 and a set of 20 produce similar results IF both are taken within a couple of reps of failure. A set of 8 at RIR 5 produces almost nothing. Proximity to failure is the variable that matters. Rep range mostly decides how unpleasant getting there is.

column1: Load cannot be faked for strength
column2: Strength is specific to heavy load. There is no rep scheme that makes a bodyweight squat build a heavy back squat. This is the hard ceiling for the 20 Rep class and 8 Time class exercises in the database, and the app should say so rather than pretend.

column1: Compounds get fewer reps and more rest
column2: A compound lift fatigues the whole system, so it goes first in the session, in a lower rep range, with longer rest. An isolation lift fatigues one muscle, so it goes last, higher reps, short rest. The Type column already carries this.

column1: Never take Power or Advanced exercises to failure
column2: A failed jump squat is a fall. A failed deadlift is a back injury. These exercises stop at RIR 2 or higher regardless of the user goal. This is a hard rule in the generator, not a suggestion.

column1: Weekly sets per muscle matter more than any single session
column2: Roughly 10 to 20 hard sets per muscle per week for growth, 6 to 10 for general fitness. The generator should budget at this level and let session structure follow, not the reverse.

column1: Fat loss does not change the lifting
column2: In a deficit, lifting exists to keep muscle. Keep the load, cut the volume, add conditioning separately. Turning the weights session into cardio is the single most common mistake and it costs the user the muscle they are trying to keep.
